# Resource upgrade

This topic describes the steps to complete the Kubernetes resource types upgrade, when a new version of Consul requires it.

## Overview

Kubernetes resources for Consul provide a way to configure and manage Consul on Kubernetes clusters. 
Consul 2.0.0 provides new resource types that offer enhanced functionality and improved compatibility with OpenShift Container Platform (OCP) versions 4.19 and above. 
These new resource types are part of the `consul.hashicorp.com` API group.

## Requirements
- A Kubernetes with Consul deployed

## Upgrade scenarios
### Kubernetes clusters with Gateway CRDs not managed by the platform:

1. Kubernetes clusters that do not use the `TCPRoute` resources.
   
   a. User can optionally deploy their API Gateway on the Consul API Gateway launched in Consul 2.0.0 by enabling flags in the helm values files. Refer to [Helm Values required on API Gateway](#helm-values-required-on-api-gateway) section.
   b. Post upgrade process. Refer to [Consul upgrade procedures](#consul-upgrade-procedures) section.

### Kubernetes clusters with Gateway CRDs managed by the platform.
 Note:  1. Currently if the platform is managing the CRDs there is high possibility that Consul installation is failed. Here we solve the problem with Consul 2.0.0
        2. Thus assumption is your cluster is in v1 where Gateway CRDs are not managed by platform but in the next version it will be managed by platform, example OCP 4.18 to OCP 4.19

1. Kubernetes clusters that do not use the `TCPRoute` resources.
    
    a. User can optionally deploy their API Gateway on the Consul API Gateway launched in Consul 2.0.0 by enabling flags in the helm values files. Refer to [Helm Values required on API Gateway](#helm-values-required-on-api-gateway) section.
    b. Post upgrade process. Refer to [Consul upgrade procedures](#consul-upgrade-procedures) section.

2. Kubernetes clusters that use the `TCPRoute` resources.
    a. User will be mandated to use the Consul API Gateway. Please refer to [Helm Values required on API Gateway](#helm-values-required-on-api-gateway) section for helm values.
    b. Upgrade process, refer to [Consul upgrade procedures](#consul-upgrade-procedures) section.

| Cluster Scenario                                                      | TCPRoute Usage                 | API Gateway Requirement                                                                                                              | Upgrade / Migration Action                                                                                                                   | Reference                |
| --------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------ |
| Kubernetes clusters with Gateway CRDs **not managed by the platform** | Not using `TCPRoute` resources | Existing API Gateway can continue to be used. Users may optionally migrate to the Consul API Gateway introduced in Consul 2.0.0. | Enable the required Helm flags to deploy the Consul API Gateway if desired. Perform the standard post-upgrade process.                   | [Helm Values](#helm-values-required-on-api-gateway), [Consul upgrade procedures](#consul-upgrade-procedures) |
| Kubernetes clusters with Gateway CRDs **managed by the platform**     | Not using `TCPRoute` resources | Existing API Gateway can continue to be used. Users may optionally migrate to the Consul API Gateway introduced in Consul 2.0.0. | Enable the required Helm flags to deploy the Consul API Gateway if desired. Perform the standard post-upgrade process.                   | [Helm Values](#helm-values-required-on-api-gateway), [Consul upgrade procedures](#consul-upgrade-procedures) |
| Kubernetes clusters with Gateway CRDs **managed by the platform**     | Using `TCPRoute` resources     | Migration to the Consul API Gateway introduced in Consul 2.0.0 is mandatory.                                                     | Deploy the consul API Gateway using the required Helm values. Follow the dedicated upgrade and migration procedure for `TCPRoute` users. | [Helm Values](#helm-values-required-on-api-gateway), [Consul upgrade procedures](#consul-upgrade-procedures) |

### Notes

1. If the platform manages Gateway CRDs, there is a high possibility that existing Consul installations may fail due to CRD ownership or reconciliation conflicts. Consul 2.0.0 addresses this issue.
2. This scenario is primarily applicable during platform upgrades where Gateway CRDs transition from being unmanaged to platform-managed, for example from OCP 4.18 to OCP 4.19.
3. The upgrade procedures assume an existing Kubernetes cluster with Consul already deployed.


## Helm Values required on API Gateway
Note:   1. For flag description, please check in [Flag Description for API Gateway](#flag-description-for-api-gateway) section.
        2. For the detail flow of upgrade check in [Consul upgrade procedures](#consul-upgrade-procedures) section.

<Tabs>
<Tab heading="Non-OCP">

<Tabs>
<Tab heading="Gateway CRDs not managed, no TCPRoute">

Kubernetes clusters with Gateway CRDs **not managed by the platform** and not using TCPRoute.

```yaml
global:
    installK8sNetworkingCRDs: true  # This will install upgraded Gateway CRDS on v1
    generateManifests: true         # This will generate manifests and applies them later on v1
    podSecurityContext:
        enabled: false              # Set this to 'true', if platform needs elevated privileges to write access on PVC.
    crds:
        enableTcpRoute: false  # Set this to 'false', disables controller to watch TCP GVK ( Group, Version, Kind)
        consulapi:
            enabled: true/false     # Set this to 'false', this disables the Consul API Gateway Controller, recommended
```

</Tab>
<Tab heading="Gateway CRDs not managed, with TCPRoute">

Kubernetes clusters with Gateway CRDs **not managed by the platform** and using TCPRoute.

```yaml
global:
    installK8sNetworkingCRDs: true  # This will install upgraded CRDS on v1
    generateManifests: true         # This will generate manifests and applies them later on v1
    podSecurityContext:
        enabled: false              # Set this to 'true', if platform needs elevated privileges to write access on PVC.
    crds:
        enableTcpRoute: true  # Set this to 'true', enables controller to watch TCP GVK ( Group, Version, Kind)
        consulapi:
            enabled: true/false     # Set this to 'false', this disables the Consul API Gateway Controller, recommended
```

</Tab>
<Tab heading="Gateway CRDs managed, no TCPRoute">

Kubernetes clusters with Gateway CRDs **managed by the platform** and not using TCPRoute.

```yaml
global:
    installK8sNetworkingCRDs: false # This will stop installation of Gateway CRDS.
    generateManifests: true         # This will generate manifests and applies them later on v1
    podSecurityContext:
        enabled: false              # Set this to 'true', if platform needs elevated privileges to write access on PVC.
    crds:
        enableTcpRoute: false  # Set this to 'false', disables controller to watch TCP GVK ( Group, Version, Kind)
        consulapi:
            enabled: true/false     # Set this to 'false', this disables the Consul API Gateway Controller, recommended
```

</Tab>
<Tab heading="Gateway CRDs managed, with TCPRoute">

Kubernetes clusters with Gateway CRDs **managed by the platform** and using TCPRoute.

```yaml
global:
    installK8sNetworkingCRDs: false # This will stop installation of Gateway CRDS.
    generateManifests: true         # This will generate manifests and applies them later on v1
    podSecurityContext:
        enabled: false              # Set this to 'true', if platform needs elevated privileges to write access on PVC.
    crds:
        enableTcpRoute: false  # Set this to 'false', disables controller to watch TCP GVK ( Group, Version, Kind)
        consulapi:
            enabled: true     # Set this to 'true', this enables the Consul API Gateway Controller, recommended
```

</Tab>
</Tabs>

Note: User can enable the Consul API Gateway all the above scenarios. Please follow the Consul upgrade procedures section.

</Tab>
<Tab heading="OCP">

<Tabs>
<Tab heading="Gateway CRDs not managed, no TCPRoute">

OCP Clusters with Gateway CRDs **not managed by the platform** and not using TCPRoute.

```yaml
global:
    installK8sNetworkingCRDs: true  # This will install upgraded Gateway CRDS on v1
    generateManifests: true         # This will generate manifests and applies them later on v1
    openshift:
        enabled: true
        upgradeTo419From418: false  # Set this to true, if OCP Cluster version is 4.18 and needs to ugrade to 4.19+, Follow Annexure 6.
        crds:
            enableTcpRoute: false   # Set this to 'false', enables controller to watch TCP GVK ( Group, Version, Kind)
            consulapi:
                enabled: false      # Set this to 'false', this disables the Consul API Gateway Controller, recommended
```

</Tab>
<Tab heading="Gateway CRDs not managed, with TCPRoute">

OCP Clusters with Gateway CRDs **not managed by the platform** and using TCPRoute.

```yaml
global:
    installK8sNetworkingCRDs: true  # This will install upgraded Gateway CRDS on v1
    generateManifests: true         # This will generate manifests and applies them later on v1
    openshift:
        enabled: true
        upgradeTo419From418: false  # Set this to true, if OCP Cluster version is 4.18 and needs to ugrade to 4.19+, Follow Annexure 6.
        crds:
            enableTcpRoute: true    # Set this to 'false', enables controller to watch TCP GVK ( Group, Version, Kind)
            consulapi:
                enabled: false      # Set this to 'false', this disables the Consul API Gateway Controller, recommended if upgradeTo419From418 is false
```

</Tab>
<Tab heading="Gateway CRDs managed, no TCPRoute">

OCP Clusters with Gateway CRDs **managed by the platform** and not using TCPRoute.

```yaml
global:
    installK8sNetworkingCRDs: false  # This will install upgraded Gateway CRDS on v1
    generateManifests: true         # This will generate manifests and applies them later on v1
    openshift:
        enabled: true
        upgradeTo419From418: false  # Set this to true, if OCP Cluster version is 4.18 and needs to ugrade to 4.19+, Follow Annexure 6.
        crds:
            enableTcpRoute: false   # Set this to 'false', disables controller to watch TCP GVK ( Group, Version, Kind)
            consulapi:
                enabled: false      # Set this to 'false', this disables the Consul API Gateway Controller, recommended
```

</Tab>
<Tab heading="Gateway CRDs managed, with TCPRoute">

OCP Clusters with Gateway CRDs **managed by the platform** and using TCPRoute.

```yaml
global:
    installK8sNetworkingCRDs: false  # This will install upgraded Gateway CRDS on v1
    generateManifests: true         # This will generate manifests and applies them later on v1
    openshift:
        enabled: true
        upgradeTo419From418: false  # Set this to true, if OCP Cluster version is 4.18 and needs to ugrade to 4.19+, Follow Annexure 6.
        crds:
            enableTcpRoute: false   # Set this to 'false', disables controller to watch TCP GVK ( Group, Version, Kind)
            consulapi:
                enabled: true      # Set this to 'true', this enables the Consul API Gateway Controller, recommended
```

</Tab>
</Tabs>

</Tab>
</Tabs>

## Consul upgrade procedures

<Tabs>
<Tab heading="Consul only upgrade - No Consul API Gateway">

### Upgrade process to 2.0.0
1. With the above helm values from the Helm Values section, set the values according to the scenario.
2. Perform `helm upgrade --install <release name> -n <namespace> -f <values.yaml>`

</Tab>
<Tab heading="Consul only upgrade - With Consul API Gateway">

### Upgrade process to 2.0.0
1. With the above helm values from the Helm Values section, set the values according to the scenario.
2. Perform `helm upgrade --install <release name> -n <namespace> -f <values.yaml>`
3. At this point, you should see both gateways up and running, while your application is still **not accessible** through **Consul API gateway** but accessible through other Standard API Gateway.
4. To access the application through the **Consul API gateway**, apply the serviceIntentions as intended. This is deliberately not done in our development.
5. Now user should be able to access the applicable through **Consul API gateway**.
6. Update your DNS with the **Consul API Gateway loadBalancer svc** and then delete the old Standard API Gateway. Example: Route53.

</Tab>
<Tab heading="Consul + Platform upgrade - No Consul API Gateway">

### Upgrade process to 2.0.0
1. With the above helm values from the Helm Values section, set the values according to the scenario.
2. Perform `helm upgrade --install <release name> -n <namespace> -f <values.yaml>`
3. Perform platform upgrade.

**Note:** The application should be accessible, if not please run the helm upgrade setting generateManifests to false.

</Tab>
<Tab heading="Consul + Platform upgrade - With Consul API Gateway">

### Upgrade process to 2.0.0
1. With the above helm values from the Helm Values section, set the values according to the scenario.
2. Perform `helm upgrade --install <release name> -n <namespace> -f <values.yaml>`
3. At this point, you should see both gateways up and running, while your application is still **not accessible** through **Consul API gateway** but accessible through other Standard API Gateway.
4. To access the application through the **Consul API gateway**, apply the serviceIntentions as intended. This is deliberately not done in our development.
5. Now user should be able to access the applicable through **Consul API gateway**.
6. Update your DNS with the **Consul API Gateway loadBalancer svc** and then delete the old Standard API Gateway. Example: Route53.
7. Perform platform upgrade.

</Tab>
</Tabs>

## OCP specific upgrade from 4.18 to 4.19

<Tabs>
<Tab heading="With TCPRoute">

You can use below values.

<Tabs>
<Tab heading="Option A: enableTcpRoute true">

```yaml
global:
    installK8sNetworkingCRDs: true
    generateManifests: true
    openshift:
        enabled: true
        upgradeTo419From418: true
        crds:
            enableTcpRoute: true
            consulapi:
                enabled: true/false
```

</Tab>
<Tab heading="Option B: enableTcpRoute false">

```yaml
global:
    installK8sNetworkingCRDs: true
    generateManifests: true
    openshift:
        enabled: true
        upgradeTo419From418: true
        crds:
            enableTcpRoute: false
            consulapi:
                enabled: false/true
```

</Tab>
</Tabs>

### Upgrade steps

1. Perform `helm upgrade --install <release name> -n <namespace> -f <values.yaml>`
2. This will generate the manifests and applies. Brings up 2 API Gateway: Standard API Gateway and Consul API Gateway.
3. At this point, you should see both gateways up and running, while your application is still **not accessible** through **Consul API gateway** but accessible through other Standard API Gateway.
4. To access the application through the **Consul API gateway**, apply the serviceIntentions as intended. This is deliberately not done in our development.
5. Now user should be able to access the applicable through **Consul API gateway**.
6. Update your DNS with the **Consul API Gateway loadBalancer svc** and then delete the old Standard API Gateway. Example: Route53.
7. Follow the OCP documentation for platform upgrade here.
    Summary steps from OCP documentation:
        1. Delete the CRDs under `gateway.networking.k8s.io` API group.
        2. Provide adminAck
        3. Delete the mutating webhook - 'consul-webhook' to avoid the upgrade failure due to consul.
        4. Perform the OCP upgrade.
8. Perform helm upgrade with the below values in the `values.yaml` file.
    ```yaml
        global:
            installK8sNetworkingCRDs: true
            generateManifests: false
            openshift:
                enabled: true
                upgradeTo419From418: false
                crds:
                    enableTcpRoute: false
                    consulapi:
                        enabled: false/true
                        # false if you want to use the standard API gateway
                        # true if you want to use the Consul API gateway
```

</Tab>
<Tab heading="No TCPRoute">


User can use the below values:

```yaml
global:
    installK8sNetworkingCRDs: true
    generateManifests: true
    openshift:
        enabled: true
        upgradeTo419From418: true
        crds:
            enableTcpRoute: false
            consulapi:
                enabled: false
```

### Upgrade steps

1. Perform `helm upgrade --install <release name> -n <namespace> -f <values.yaml>`
2. This will generate the manifests and applies the gateway objects under v1 version of the API group 'gateway.networking.k8s.io'
3. Follow the OCP documentation for platform upgrade here.
4. After the platform upgrade, application should be accessible, if not continue next steps.

### Post-upgrade configuration (if needed)

User can use the below values:

```yaml
global:
    installK8sNetworkingCRDs: false
    generateManifests: false
    openshift:
        enabled: true
        upgradeTo419From418: false
        crds:
            enableTcpRoute: false
            consulapi:
                enabled: false
```

6. Perform `helm upgrade --install <release name> -n <namespace> -f <values.yaml>`
7. Application should be accessible

</Tab>
</Tabs>
                


## Flag description for API Gateway

1. **`installK8sNetworkingCRDs`**: `true`/`false` (boolean)
    a. User can install the Gateway CRDs from Kubernetes.io; Or if the Gateway CRDs are managed by the platform, User can set this to `false`.
    b. If Consul should manage the Gateway CRDs set this to `true`

2. **`generateManifests`**: `true`/`false` (boolean)
    a. If `false`, we don't run the job to generate the manifests for the Gateway and Gateway related Objects.
    b. If `true`, we generate manifests depending on the flags.

3. **`enableTcpRoute`** and **`crds.consulapi.enabled`**:
    
    During the install:
    - **`enableTcpRoute`** installs the TCP GVK under `gateway.networking.k8s.io` and enables the Standard gateway controller to watch for the resources.
    - **`crds.consulapi.enabled`** installs the CRDs under `consul.hashicorp.com` API group and enables the Consul API Gateway controller to watch for the resources.

    During the upgrade - manifests are generated:
    - If **`consulapi.enabled`** is set to `false`; no manifests will be generated under `consul.hashicorp.com` API group
    - If **`consulapi.enabled`** is set to `true`; manifests will be generated and applied under `consul.hashicorp.com` API group
    
    ```yaml
    crds:
        enableTcpRoute: true
        consulapi:
            enabled: false
    ```

4. **`upgradeTo419From418`**: `true`/`false` (boolean)
    
    During the install:
    - In this case ideally customer deploys Consul directly on OCP 4.19+ and thus is void.

    During the upgrade:
    - This should be enabled when the customer plans to upgrade OCP from 4.18 to 4.19+ versions.
    - This will generate the manifests in accordance to 2 cases along with **`enableTcpRoute`**:
      - **Case 1**: when **`upgradeTo419From418`** is `true` and **`enableTcpRoute`** is `false`:
        Generate the manifests only for `gateway.networking.k8s.io` and ignores the TCP watch
      - **Case 2**: when **`upgradeTo419From418`** is `true` and **`enableTcpRoute`** is `true`:
        Generate the manifests for both `consul.hashicorp.com` and `gateway.networking.k8s.io` (the deletion of TCP GVK is manual)
    
    ```yaml
    upgradeTo419From418: false
    ```

5. **`isOcpGreaterthan4_18`**: `true`/`false` (boolean)
    
    During the install:
    - In this case ideally customer deploys Consul directly on OCP 4.19+ and thus is void.
   
    - For **`crds.enableTcpRoute`** set to `true`; controller watches for the TCP
    - For **`crds.enableTcpRoute`** set to `false`; controller does not watch for the TCP
    - For **`crds.consulapi.enabled`** set to `true`; controller watches for resources under the API group `consul.hashicorp.com`
 
    During the upgrade:
    - If **`isOcpGreaterthan4_18`** is `false` and **`enableTcpRoute`** is `false`:
        Generate the manifests only for `gateway.networking.k8s.io` and ignores the TCP watch
    - If **`isOcpGreaterthan4_18`** is `false` and **`enableTcpRoute`** is `true`:
        Generate the manifests only for `gateway.networking.k8s.io` and watches for the TCP
    - If **`isOcpGreaterthan4_18`** is `true` and **`enableTcpRoute`** is `false`:
        Generate the manifests only for `gateway.networking.k8s.io` and ignores the TCP watch
    - If **`isOcpGreaterthan4_18`** is `true` and **`enableTcpRoute`** is `true`:
        Generate the manifests for both `consul.hashicorp.com` (all current GVK) and `gateway.networking.k8s.io` (no TCP)

    In any case, **`crds.consulapi.enabled`** installs the CRDs under `consul.hashicorp.com` API group and enables the Consul API Gateway controller to watch for the resources.
    
    Example configuration:
    
    ```yaml
    isOcpGreaterthan4_18: false  # Flag to indicate OCP version
    crds:
      enableTcpRoute: false       # Flag to enable/disable TCP route watching
      consulapi:
        enabled: false            # Flag to enable/disable Consul API gateway
    ```
Note: for more scenarios, please check in the values.yaml or contact support.
